top_gaps: It establishes the collapse phenomenon but leaves open practical mitigation strategies and conditions under which collapse can be delayed or avoided.
It demonstrates severe collapse consequences in a safety-critical setting but does not yet offer a general mitigation framework or cross-domain theory.
It explores synthetic self-improvement but leaves unresolved when such gains reliably generalize without incurring long-run diversity collapse.
It offers stabilization ideas but does not fully characterize the boundary between robust self-correction and eventual recursive degradation in realistic web-scale settings.
It clearly diagnoses recursive collapse but leaves open how much curated real data or filtering is sufficient to prevent it in practice.
It gives a preference-oriented theoretical rescue of self-consuming loops but leaves unanswered how robust its assumptions are for noisy real-world web contamination.
It proposes a practical anti-collapse fine-tuning technique but does not yet establish whether the approach scales across modalities or long iterative retraining chains.
It adds a new collapse-sensitive metric and mitigation idea but leaves open how surplexity compares to standard quality and diversity measures across settings.
It identifies the LLM-specific recursive contamination problem but leaves unresolved the empirical thresholds at which web-scale synthetic prevalence triggers severe collapse.
It clarifies theoretical prevention conditions but still leaves a gap between abstract guarantees and operational dataset policies for real web-scale retraining.
It documents prevalence in scientific literature but does not directly connect contamination rates to downstream model training or retrieval degradation.
It offers broad prevalence estimates but lacks peer-reviewed methodology and does not assess training-corpus consequences or temporal dynamics in depth.
It suggests filtering can preserve quality but does not provide a full curation framework or quantify effects on downstream model behavior.
It articulates the failure mode conceptually but leaves open precise prevalence thresholds and validated long-term empirical measurements across retrieval ecosystems.
It focuses on educational responses rather than the open web and does not connect detected prevalence to model-training contamination or broader web-scale pollution.
It articulates the failure mode conceptually but leaves open precise prevalence thresholds and validated long-term empirical measurements across retrieval ecosystems.
It depends on detector-based estimation in one language/domain and does not establish direct effects on web-scale training corpora or retrieval systems.
It provides domain-specific prevalence evidence but leaves unresolved how robust the estimates are and how they translate to broader web contamination or model-training risk.
It is broad and likely less rigorous than empirical prevalence studies, leaving uncertain quantitative estimates of AI-content share and direct relevance to training data pollution.
It focuses on one platform and image modality, leaving open how AI-generated content fractions evolve across broader web corpora or text-heavy training data sources.
It studies user reactions to labeling rather than technical detector performance or the actual prevalence of AI-generated content online.
It provides behavioral evidence about labels but not robust methods for detecting unlabeled AI content or estimating contamination at scale.
It is a task-specific detector paper and does not address detector robustness, watermarking limits, or broader web-scale contamination measurement.
It proposes watermarking but leaves unresolved how robust such schemes remain under paraphrasing, editing, or broader adversarial transformations.
It demonstrates detector fragility but does not fully solve scalable detection for novel, heavily transformed, or retrieval-unavailable generations.
Its perplexity-based approach is domain-specific and may not be robust to paraphrase-like transformations, mixed authorship, or stronger future code models.

It synthesizes detector methods broadly but does not provide a focused quantitative account of long-run robustness against paraphrasing, adversarial editing, or deployment shift in text-only settings.
It sharpens understanding of detectability factors but does not solve provenance or watermarking-based identification for robust real-world use.
It covers watermarking broadly across media but is not centered on the specific limitations of LLM text detection or resilient text watermark deployment.
It documents detector unreliability in one application domain but does not establish generalizable mitigation strategies or robust provenance mechanisms.

It identifies diversity degradation under synthetic-data recursion but does not fully connect those linguistic metrics to broader collapse dynamics such as tail-risk amplification or downstream capability loss.
It evaluates synthetic samples at the distribution and memorization level but does not address recursive training dynamics or text-specific web-scale quality degradation.
It measures corpus properties but does not connect these metrics to synthetic text contamination, recursive training, or longitudinal web-quality change.
It formalizes diversity metrics across corpora but does not apply them to synthetic-data degeneration or AI-generated web pollution.
It provides metric foundations for corpus diversity without linking them to model collapse, provenance, or training-data curation decisions.
Its use of perplexity and KL is model-theoretic rather than about corpus quality, synthetic contamination, or recursive degradation in training data.
It informs metric stability but does not directly study synthetic text, language-model data curation, or temporal web-quality degradation.
It measures lexical diversity in learner writing but does not connect the metric to AI-generated text, web-scale corpora, or LLM training quality.
It analyzes linguistic properties of translated training data, but stops short of deriving concrete curation policies for large-scale pretraining mixtures.
It clarifies evaluability in theory, but does not provide text-quality metrics or empirical guidance for detecting synthetic-data degradation in practice.
Although comprehensive on pre-training mixtures, it leaves open how its findings extend to repeated self-consuming loops over many generations.
It is centered on vision continual learning rather than measuring web-scale contamination rates or text-data curation for language model pretraining.
It does not clearly isolate which specific curation choices or source proportions drive downstream gains.
It leaves open how well its filtering criteria generalize across languages and scales to larger multilingual pretraining regimes.
The paper is not primarily a controlled study of data curation, so it provides limited ablation on how individual data-quality interventions affect performance.
It questions CQF but does not fully resolve what scalable alternative quality-selection method should replace it across domains.
It does not fully address long-run risks of recursive synthetic-data training or the provenance tradeoffs versus authentic human-produced corpora.
It identifies transferability problems in proxy-model practice but does not offer a definitive universally reliable protocol for data-recipe selection.
It foregrounds extraction quality but leaves open how its parsing advantages compare over time and across broader web-quality measurement frameworks.
It leaves unresolved how robust its quality-diversity tradeoff is across model scales, domains, and changing token budgets.
It identifies a social-capability trade-off but does not establish how to collect higher-quality authentic human social data that avoids these failures.
It reduces dependence on human supervision but does not fully characterize when minimal-supervision alignment matches or fails against richer human preference data.
It provides valuable human preference data but does not directly address annotation cost reduction or compare human data value against synthetic substitutes.
It simplifies RLHF training but still depends on preference data and does not eliminate broader human-data collection and quality bottlenecks.
It proposes a lower-supervision alignment strategy but does not settle how synthetic or self-generated preference signals compare with authentic human feedback at scale.
As a survey, it frames the design space but does not empirically quantify the comparative value of human versus synthetic feedback in language model alignment.
It streamlines preference-based alignment but still assumes access to preference data and does not solve data sourcing, cost, or representativeness issues.
It does not resolve when AI feedback fails relative to genuinely diverse human preference data or how quality degrades under recursive synthetic preference generation.
As a survey, it synthesizes rather than newly quantifies the comparative value, scarcity, and failure modes of human versus synthetic preference data.
It identifies a failure mode of feedback-driven alignment but does not provide a comprehensive framework for valuing or replacing human data in RLHF pipelines.
It offers model provenance marking, but does not solve ecosystem-level data provenance, governance, or robust detection against adaptive removal attacks.
It demonstrates large-scale behavioral data capture but does not address AI-training provenance standards, consent governance, or contamination-aware data authenticity for modern model pipelines.
It focuses on sensing and inference rather than provenance metadata, contributor rights, or AI-data governance mechanisms for downstream training use.
It offers a legal-policy framing rather than empirical measurement of contamination, operational provenance infrastructure, or technical validation methods for authenticity.
It is primarily a position statement and does not fully specify deployable provenance architectures or benchmarked remediation mechanisms.
It emphasizes technical traceability but does not establish broad adoption evidence, contributor-centric governance, or strong evaluation on authentic human-data ecosystems.
It surfaces governance problems but does not provide a concrete provenance infrastructure or AI-training-oriented pipeline design.
It clarifies governance tensions but does not specify technical provenance mechanisms or validation standards for AI training datasets.
It focuses on biomedical data-sharing trade-offs rather than platform design for collecting or certifying authentic human-generated training data at scale.
It addresses provenance for measurement instruments rather than contributor data collection or AI-training dataset authenticity directly.
It improves web-data curation at scale but does not directly measure temporal degradation of web quality or AI-generated content contamination over time.
It provides web-scale quality selection infrastructure but stops short of directly quantifying AI-content prevalence or broader temporal quality decline on the web.
It documents one major corpus in depth but does not track web-text quality longitudinally across time or quantify synthetic-content pollution.
Because it relies on imperfect detectors and a single platform, it cannot fully establish true prevalence trends or contamination levels in broader web training corpora.
It measures temporal change in a specific web genre rather than quality shifts in general web training data or AI-generated content contamination.
It analyzes market concentration in online activity rather than linguistic quality, provenance, or synthetic contamination of web text used for AI training.
It measures shifts in consent and governance signals but does not directly quantify resulting text-quality changes or downstream effects on model training outcomes.
It offers longitudinal measurement of web-document evolution but is not focused on AI-training corpora or direct training-data quality implications.
It characterizes temporal change in the web broadly but does not connect those shifts to AI-data contamination, text quality, or training curation decisions.
It identifies stage-specific effects on social competence but does not isolate which data sources or composition choices drive the observed gains and failures.
It sets evaluation standards but does not itself provide a new benchmark result on social reasoning or evidence about training-data interventions.
It reveals stage-specific competence changes but does not disentangle whether the effects come from data composition, objective choice, or model scale.
It measures ToM performance but does not provide a new benchmark or training dataset for improving social reasoning with authentic human interaction data.
It demonstrates the value of human behavioral signals for social reasoning but does not establish a standardized benchmark or scalable collection pipeline for such signals.
It provides a foundational benchmark for social commonsense but is limited to text-based multiple-choice evaluation rather than richer interactive or behavioral social reasoning.
It diagnoses weaknesses in LLM social reasoning but does not offer a broad multimodal or behaviorally grounded remedy for those limitations.
It broadens benchmark coverage but still centers on evaluation rather than collecting real-world human social interaction data for training.
It motivates human-like social reasoning but does not by itself resolve how to gather large-scale, ecologically valid social data for training and evaluation.
It demonstrates synthetic instruction tuning at scale but does not isolate fine-tuning composition effects against carefully curated human data on social reasoning tasks.
It opens human preference data collection and alignment pipelines but does not cleanly separate how data quality, provenance, and composition each affect downstream model behavior.
It shows the power of curated small-scale alignment data but does not provide systematic composition ablations across human, synthetic, and socially grounded datasets.
It advances synthetic data generation for instruction tuning but does not compare synthetic versus human social data in a targeted social-reasoning ablation setting.
It does not directly establish how its selection strategy generalizes across domains, model sizes, or real-vs-synthetic social data settings.
It does not clarify which properties of the human data drive gains or whether improvements transfer beyond domain-specific response quality to broader social reasoning.
It leaves open whether synthetic empathy data supports deeper social reasoning or only surface-level empathetic response generation.
It does not quantify the cost-benefit tradeoff of post-editing at scale or isolate which edits matter most for broader instruction-tuning performance.
It does not quantify the cost-benefit tradeoff of post-editing at scale or isolate which edits matter most for broader instruction-tuning performance.
It does not cleanly disentangle the effects of data quality, diversity, and quantity or compare human-authored against synthetic alternatives in controlled ablations.
It is outside the thesis taxonomy because it addresses RL reasoning efficiency rather than recursive synthetic training, web pollution, provenance, or data quality in the relevant sense.
It is outside the thesis taxonomy because it concerns medical imaging domain generalization rather than generative-model collapse, web-text quality, provenance, or training-data curation for LLMs.
It is about domain-specific harms of synthetic visual media rather than measuring web-text contamination, training data quality, or collapse dynamics in the thesis taxonomy.
It addresses misinformation psychology rather than web pollution measurement, provenance, detection methods, or training-data quality.
It is informative about behavioral evaluation of LLMs but does not substantially address human feedback value, provenance, data quality, collapse, or related thesis categories.
It is interpretive rather than a measurement study of web-text quality, synthetic-content prevalence, provenance systems, or AI-training data curation.
It focuses on event extraction from Wikipedia dynamics rather than temporal web-quality measurement, provenance, or AI-generated content prevalence.
It does not address the thesis themes of web-data quality, provenance, model collapse, or social-reasoning benchmarks in LLMs.
It does not address training-data mixture, curation, or synthetic-versus-human fine-tuning ablations central to the thesis taxonomy.
